events.note: The Games are on. This one is useful year-round, but it earns its keep this fortnight.

es.meaning: To measure up; to be good enough for the occasion.
es.example: No dio la talla en la final.
es.exampleTranslation: He didn't measure up in the final.

it.meaning: To measure up; to be equal to the task.
it.example: Non è stato all'altezza della finale.
it.exampleTranslation: He wasn't up to the final.

bridge.note: Both reach for measurement, but along different axes: Spanish measures your SIZE (talla, as in clothing), Italian measures your HEIGHT. And the registers part company — dar la talla is colloquial and would cost you marks in a DELE oral, while essere all'altezza is neutral enough for the CILS.

interference.es_to_it: 'Dare la taglia' is not Italian — taglia is a clothing size and nothing else. Use essere all'altezza.
interference.it_to_es: 'Estar a la altura' does exist in Spanish and is the safer, more formal choice; dar la talla is its colloquial cousin.

review.notes: Authored as a schema demo; sources not human-verified; reviewer sign-off pending.
